perturbation.original_text: The initial term of the Agreement is three (3) years. If the Agreement is not terminated by notice six (6) months prior to the end of each term, it shall be extended with twelve (12) months with the same notice period until terminated as aforesaid by any Party.
perturbation.changed_text: The initial term of the Agreement is three (3) years. If the Agreement is not terminated by notice one (1) month prior to the end of each term, it shall be extended with twelve (12) months with the same notice period until terminated as aforesaid by any Party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the termination notice period from six months to one month could be problematic in jurisdictions where a longer notice period is legally required or considered standard practice for contracts of this nature.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 3(1) states that a contractual term which has not been individually negotiated shall be regarded as unfair if, contrary to the requirement of good faith, it causes a significant imbalance in the parties' rights and obligations arising under the contract, to the detriment of the consumer. A one-month notice period may be deemed insufficient and thus unfair, especially in a business context where Haldex is a larger and more established company. While this agreement isn't explicitly with a consumer, the principle of fairness can be applied by analogy in some jurisdictions. Sweden implements this directive through its own consumer protection laws and contract law principles, and a Swedish court could deem a one-month notice period as unreasonably short in some circumstances, especially if it significantly disadvantages the supplier.

perturbation.justification: The change from a six-month to a one-month termination notice period introduces a potential legal contradiction. The Law Explanation suggests that such a short notice period might be deemed unfair, particularly if it significantly disadvantages one party, drawing an analogy from consumer protection laws. The modified text weakens the original protection afforded by a longer notice period and could be seen as violating the principle of fairness. Although the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the Law Explanation, coupled with the original and changed text and the perturbation explanation, points to a scenario where the modified clause might not withstand legal scrutiny.

perturbation.original_text: Any and all disputes, controversies and claims arising out of or in connection with this Agreement, or the breach, termination or invalidity hereof, shall be settled by arbitration in accordance with the Rules of the Arbitration Institute of th e Stockholm Chamber of Commerce.
perturbation.changed_text: Any and all disputes, controversies and claims arising out of or in connection with this Agreement, or the breach, termination or invalidity hereof, shall be settled by arbitration in accordance with rules determined solely by Haldex.
perturbation.explanation: Specifying that arbitration rules are determined solely by Haldex may violate principles of fairness and impartiality required in arbitration proceedings.
perturbation.law_citation: Section 17 of the Swedish Arbitration Act
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 17 of the Swedish Arbitration Act states that arbitrators shall be impartial and independent. Granting one party (Haldex) the sole right to determine the arbitration rules could be interpreted as creating an imbalance and undermining the impartiality of the arbitration process, potentially leading to the arbitration agreement being deemed unenforceable under Swedish law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: eller någon närstående till skiljemannen är ledamot av styrelsen för ett bolag eller någon annan sammanslutning som är part eller annars är ställföreträdare för en part eller för någon annan som kan vänta beaktansvärd nytta eller skada av tvistens utgång, 3. om skiljemannen som sakkunnig eller annars har tagit ställning i tvisten eller hjälpt en part att förbereda eller utföra sin talan i tvisten, eller 4. om skiljemannen har mottagit eller förbehållit sig ersättning i strid med 39 § andra stycket. Lag (2018:1954) . 9 § Den som ombeds att åta sig ett uppdrag som skiljeman skall genast uppge alla omständigheter som enligt 7 eller 8 § kan tänkas hindra den vidtalade från att vara skiljeman. En skiljeman skall upplysa parterna och övriga skiljemän om sådana omständigheter så snart alla skiljemän har utsetts och därefter under skiljeförfarandet så snart skiljemannen har fått kännedom om någon ny omständighet. 10 § Ett yrkande om att en skiljeman skall skiljas från sitt uppdrag på grund av en omständighet som anges i 8 § skall framställas inom femton dagar från det att parten fick kännedom såväl om att skiljemannen har utsetts som om omständigheten. Yrkandet prövas av skiljemännen, om parterna inte har bestämt att det skall prövas av någon annan. Bifalls yrkandet får beslutet inte angripas. En part som är missnöjd med ett beslut genom vilket ett yrkande ogillats eller avvisats som för sent framställt, får hos tingsrätten ansöka om att skiljemannen skall skiljas från uppdraget. Ansökan skall göras inom trettio dagar från den dag då parten fick del av beslutet. Skiljemännen får fortsätta skiljeförfarandet i avvaktan på tingsrättens avgörande. 11 § Parterna får bestämma att ett yrkande som anges i 10 § första stycket skall prövas slutligt av ett skiljedomsinstitut. 12 § Parterna får bestämma hur många skiljemännen skall vara och hur de skall utses. Om parterna inte har bestämt något annat gäller 13-16 §§. Tingsrätten skall utse skiljemän även i andra fall än som anges i 14-17 §§, om parterna har bestämt det och någon av parterna ansöker om det. 13 § Skiljemännen skall vara tre. Vardera parten väljer en skiljeman och de valda utser den tredje. 14 § Om parterna var för sig ska välja skiljeman och den ena parten i en begäran om skiljedom enligt 19 § har underrättat motparten om sitt val av skiljeman, är motparten skyldig att inom trettio dagar från mottagandet av underrättelsen skriftligen underrätta den första parten om sitt val av skiljeman. En part som på detta sätt har underrättat motparten om sitt val av skiljeman får inte återkalla valet utan motpartens samtycke. Om motparten låter bli att utse skiljeman inom den angivna tiden, ska tingsrätten på ansökan av den första parten utse skiljeman. Om ett skiljeförfarande har påkallats mot flera motparter och dessa inte kan enas om valet av skiljeman, ska tingsrätten på ansökan av en motpart, inom den frist som anges i första stycket, utse skiljemän för alla parter och samtidigt skilja en redan utsedd skiljeman från uppdraget. Lag (2018:1954) . 15 § Skall en skiljeman utses av andra skiljemän men låter de bli att göra det inom trettio dagar från det att den sista av dem blev vald, skall tingsrätten på ansökan av en part utse skiljemannen. Skall en skiljeman utses av någon annan än en part eller skiljemän, men görs inte det inom trettio dagar från det att en part som önskar att skiljeman utses uppmanat den som skall utse skiljeman att göra det, skall tingsrätten på ansökan av en part utse skiljemannen. Detsamma gäller, om en skiljeman skall utses av parterna gemensamt men de inte har enats inom trettio dagar från det att frågan väckts genom att den ena parten mottagit en underrättelse från motparten. 16 § Om en skiljeman avgår eller skiljs från uppdraget på grund av omständigheter som förelåg när skiljemannen utsågs, ska tingsrätten på ansökan av en part utse en ny skiljeman. Om skiljemannen utsågs av en part ska tingsrätten, om inte särskilda skäl talar emot det, utse den person som parten f
perturbation.justification: The changed text, by granting Haldex sole determination of arbitration rules, contradicts principles of impartiality required in arbitration. The Law Explanation states this could undermine the impartiality of the arbitration process. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions circumstances that can hinder someone from being an arbitrator (e.g., being on the board of a company involved in the dispute), implying the need for impartiality. While Snippet 1 doesn't directly address rule-setting, the broad implication is that arbitrators and the arbitration process should be free from undue influence. Giving one party sole control over the rules creates an imbalance that violates this principle. The original text refers to the 'Rules of the Arbitration Institute of th e Stockholm Chamber of Commerce,' ensuring a neutral standard, while the changed text removes this safeguard. This represents a significant and enforceable contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the substantive laws of Sweden.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the substantive laws of Delaware.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from Sweden to Delaware when the arbitration takes place in Stockholm, Sweden and the contract is between a Swedish entity and a supplier can create conflict of laws issues and potentially violate Swedish mandatory law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 3 of the Rome I Regulation addresses freedom of choice. However, it also acknowledges limitations imposed by mandatory rules of the country where all other elements relevant to the situation at the time the choice is made are located. Since the agreement primarily involves entities and activities within Sweden, Swedish mandatory laws could override the choice of Delaware law if applying Delaware law would circumvent fundamental principles of Swedish law.

perturbation.justification: The change from Swedish law to Delaware law as the governing law creates a potential conflict. The Law Explanation states that Article 3 of the Rome I Regulation acknowledges limitations imposed by mandatory rules of the country where all other elements relevant to the situation are located. The Perturbation Explanation mentions that the arbitration takes place in Stockholm, Sweden, and the contract is between a Swedish entity and a supplier, which could mean Swedish law should apply, thus indicating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: vi) to ascertain that each person who receives Confidential Information is made aware of and comply with this Agreement;
perturbation.changed_text: vi) to make a reasonable effort to ensure each person who receives Confidential Information is made aware of and complies with this Agreement;
perturbation.explanation: Changing the obligation from 'ascertain' to 'make a reasonable effort' could weaken the enforceability of the confidentiality provisions. If a data breach occurs due to an employee's non-compliance, the 'reasonable effort' standard may not meet the requirements of GDPR if personal data is involved.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 28(3)(b) of GDPR requires controllers to impose obligations on processors to implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk. While this agreement is about confidentiality generally, if confidential information includes personal data, then 'reasonable effort' may not be considered sufficient to meet the requirement of implementing 'appropriate' measures, especially if stronger measures were reasonably available.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Data protection - European Commission Skip to main content Resources Resources Access a range of resources, including publications, statistics, learning material, and tools for stakeholders. Publications Statistics Search Eurostat statistics Economic forecasts and trends Tracking EU policy performance and recovery Public opinion survey EU open data portal Guide to European statistics See all Eurobarometer EU law Learning corner Europa Web Guide Design, content and development Test, monitor, measure and report Additional resources Governance of the Europa Domain Planning your web presence See all European Commission visual identity Discover more Europe and you Get involved Share your views on EU laws and policies, debate Europe's future and find funding for your EU projects. Engage in EU policymaking Have your say European Citizens’ Panels European Citizens' Initiative Petition the EU See all Jobs Funding and tenders Events Visit us Visitors' Centre Experience Europe exhibition centre See all European Commission on social media Discover more FEATURED Flying the European flag since 1985 Data protection Rules for the protection of personal data inside and outside the EU. Data protection in the EU In today's digital age, where information is constantly shared, collected and processed, there is a need for clear and strong data protection rules. Data protection is a fundamental right under EU law. EU data protection legislation is comprised of the General Data Protection Directive (GDPR), the Law Enforcement Directive (LED), and the Data Protection Regulation for EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies (EUDPR). To ensure that this legislation is applied consistently, national and European data protection authorities and bodies have been established. Find out more about the EU's legal framework for data protection Data protection: questions and answers Data protection explained Read about key concepts such as personal data, data processing, when and to whom the GDPR applies to, and more. Information for individuals Read about the rights you have over your personal data under the GDPR, how to exercise these rights, and more. Information for businesses and organisations Read about data protection principles and obligations, enforcement of the rules, dealing with individuals' requests, and more. Disclaimer The information and guidance in these webpages are intended to contribute to a better understanding of EU data protection rules. This is intended purely as a guidance tool – only the text of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) has legal force. As a consequence, only the GDPR is liable to create rights and obligations for individuals. This guidance does not create any enforceable right or expectation. The binding interpretation of EU legislation is the exclusive competence of the Court of Justice of the European Union. The views expressed in this guidance are without prejudice to the position that the Commission might take before the Court of Justice. Neither the European Commission nor any person acting on behalf of the European Commission is responsible for the use which might be made of the following information. As this guidance reflects the state of the art at the time of its drafting, it should be regarded as a 'living tool' open for improvement and its content may be subject to modifications without notice. International dimension of data protection The EU has established international data protection agreements to ensure that EU citizens' personal data remains protected even if transferred outside the EU. EU data protection legislation includes safeguards for when transferring data to third countries, including adequacy decisions, standard contractual clauses (SCC) and binding corporate rules (BCR). Find out more about the international dimension of data protection Funding to support the implementation of the GDPR The Commission has provided funding to national data protection authorities to finance projects tha
perturbation.justification: The change from "ascertain" to "make a reasonable effort" weakens the obligation to ensure compliance with the agreement. If confidential information includes personal data covered by GDPR, this change could be a problem. The Law Explanation notes that Article 28(3)(b) of GDPR requires appropriate technical and organizational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk. The changed text, which mandates only a "reasonable effort," may be deemed insufficient to meet GDPR's requirement of implementing 'appropriate' measures, especially if stronger measures were reasonably available. The European Commission's page on Data protection describes the importance of clear and strong data protection rules, as data protection is a fundamental right under EU law. By weakening the obligation to ensure compliance, the modified text potentially undermines these data protection rules, especially if the confidential information includes personal data, creating a contradiction. While the snippet is very general, it demonstrates the high priority placed on data protection by the EU.

perturbation.original_text: vii) to immediately upon request deliver to the other Party all Confidential Information that belong to that P arty in any form without retaining any copies thereof, or, at the other Party’s option, certify in writing that all copies of such information have been destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: vii) to deliver to the other Party all Confidential Information that belongs to that Party in any form within 90 days of request without retaining any copies thereof, or, at the other Party’s option, certify in writing that all copies of such information have been destroyed.
perturbation.explanation: Introducing a 90-day delay in returning confidential information upon request could contradict trade secret laws if such a delay would allow for misappropriation or unauthorized use of the information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Directive (EU) 2016/943 on the protection of undisclosed know-how and business information (trade secrets) against their unlawful acquisition, use and disclosure
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 4(2) of the Directive (EU) 2016/943 mandates member states to ensure that competent judicial authorities may, on application by the trade secret holder, order the cessation of or prohibit the use or disclosure of the trade secret. A 90-day delay could significantly diminish the trade secret holder's ability to prevent unlawful use during that period, potentially weakening the protection intended by this law and in some circumstances enabling disclosure that would otherwise be prevented. In the case of Sweden this directive is implemented in the Lag (2018:558) om företagshemligheter (Law on Trade Secrets).

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 90-day delay in returning confidential information upon request, which contradicts the intent of Directive (EU) 2016/943. The directive, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, aims to protect undisclosed know-how and business information (trade secrets) against unlawful acquisition, use, and disclosure. The 90-day delay significantly weakens this protection, as it provides a window for potential misappropriation or unauthorized use of the confidential information, contrary to the directive's purpose.
